saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them hesitate and whisper to each other. "You ask her, Niel."

"You should do it, George. She visits your house often, but she hardly knows me."

They stopped at the three steps leading up to the porch just as Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door, nodding graciously, a pink rose in her hand.

"Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic?"

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly removed his large straw hat. "Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Of course. I hope you have a lovely day. How long has school been out? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does—Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite the scholar."

The boys laughed, but Niel looked uneasy.

"Run along now, and remember to keep the pasture gate closed. Mr. Forrester dislikes having the cattle in his blue grass."

Quietly, the boys circled the house to the grove gate, then broke into a run, shouting as they dashed down the grassy slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched them until they vanished over the crest of the hill. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, when you bake this morning, please make a batch of cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime."

They halted at the three steps leading to the porch just as Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, nodding politely with a pink rose in her hand.

Silently, the boys skirted the house to the grove gate, then broke into a sprint, calling out as they raced down the grassy slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched them until they disappeared over the hilltop. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

"Mary, when you bake this morning, please prepare a batch of cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime."

saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she saw them stop and whisper to each other. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You should do it, George. She knows you since she often visits your house. She hardly knows me.”

They paused at the three steps leading up to the porch just as Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, nodding politely, a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward, solemnly removing his wide straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. I hope you have a lovely day. School’s been out for a while now, hasn’t it? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does—Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite the scholar.”

The boys laughed softly, but Niel looked uncomfortable.

“Now off you go, and don’t forget to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle in his bluegrass.”

Without another word, the boys circled the house to the grove gate and broke into a run, shouting as they raced down the grassy slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the hill. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please make a batch of cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she noticed them halt and exchange whispered words. “You ask her, Niel.”

“It should be you, George. She knows you—she’s always at your house. She hardly even knows me.”

They hesitated at the three steps leading to the porch just as Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, nodding graciously with a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic today?”

George Adams stepped forward, solemnly doffing his wide straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. I hope you have a wonderful time. School’s been out for some time now, hasn’t it? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does—Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite the scholar.”

The boys chuckled softly, but Niel seemed uneasy.

“Now off you go, and remember to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle in his bluegrass.”

Without another word, they skirted the house to the grove gate and broke into a run, calling out as they raced down the shaded slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the hill. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please prepare a batch of cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys ascended the hill, she noticed them pause and exchange quiet words. “You ask her, Niel.”

“It should be you, George. She knows you—she’s always at your house. She hardly knows me.”

They hesitated at the three steps leading to the porch just as Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, greeting them warmly with a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic today?”

George Adams stepped forward, respectfully removing his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. I hope you enjoy yourselves. School’s been out for some time now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I imagine Niel does—Judge Pommeroy says he’s quite the scholar.”

The boys exchanged quiet laughter, but Niel appeared uneasy.

“Well, off you go, and don’t forget to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his bluegrass.”

Without further words, they skirted the house, slipped through the grove gate, and broke into a run, calling to each other as they dashed down the shaded slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the hill. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please prepare a batch of cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime.”

George Adams stepped forward and politely removed his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh, and have lunch in the grove?”

saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she noticed them pause briefly, exchanging quiet words.

“You ask her, Niel.”

“No, you do it, George. She knows you—you’re always at her house. I hardly know her at all.”

They lingered at the foot of the three porch steps while Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway, a pink rose in her hand and a warm smile on her face.

“Good morning, boys. Are you setting off for a picnic today?”

George Adams stepped forward, respectfully lifting his wide straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. I hope you enjoy yourselves. School’s been out for some time now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I daresay Niel might—he’s quite the scholar, Judge Pommeroy tells me.”

The boys chuckled, but Niel looked uncomfortable.

“Well, off you go, and don’t forget to shut the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want his bluegrass trampled by cattle.”

Quietly, they skirted the house, slipped through the grove gate, and broke into a run, calling to each other as they raced down the shady slope beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they vanished over the hill. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please make some cookies for those boys. I’ll bring them down at lunchtime.”

The boys chuckled, but Niel seemed uneasy.

“Well, enjoy yourselves, but remember to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his bluegrass.”

They quietly skirted the house, slipped through the gate into the grove, and broke into a run, calling to each other as they raced down the shaded hillside beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the crest. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please make some cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime.”
saida_filtrada:
As the boys ascended the hill, she saw them stop for a moment, whispering to each other.

“You ask her, Niel.”

“No, you do it, George. She knows you—you’re always at her place. I barely know her.”

They hesitated at the base of the porch steps while Mrs. Forrester emerged in the doorway, holding a pink rose and offering a welcoming smile.

“Good morning, boys. Are you off for a picnic today?”

George Adams stepped forward, politely removing his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we go fishing and wading in the marsh, then have lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. I hope you have a wonderful time. School’s been out for a while now, hasn’t it? Do you miss it? I suspect Niel might—he’s quite the scholar, Judge Pommeroy tells me.”

The boys chuckled, but Niel seemed uneasy.

“Well, enjoy yourselves, but remember to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester doesn’t want the cattle trampling his bluegrass.”

They quietly skirted the house, slipped through the gate into the grove, and broke into a run, calling to each other as they raced down the shaded hillside beneath the tall trees. From the kitchen window, Mrs. Forrester watched until they disappeared over the crest. Turning to her Bohemian cook, she said, “Mary, when you bake this morning, please make some cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime.”
